_purpose: County Landscape Profile for Greene County, IA. Physical geography, sensory snapshots, and landscape change narratives for the southeastern anchor of the Corn Belt cluster. Greene County is Des Moines Lobe terrain with the North Raccoon River crossing the county. Jefferson is the county seat — a classic Iowa county-seat town with a courthouse square. The county attracted Yankee, German, and Scandinavian settlers. The Raccoon River valley provides the primary geographic feature, and the county's agricultural economy was corn, livestock, and dairy.

physical_geography.physiographic_province: Des Moines Lobe — gently rolling glacial terrain. Greene County lies on the Des Moines Lobe with slightly more relief than the flatter counties to the north (Calhoun, Sac). The Raccoon River system provides the primary drainage and topographic variety.
physical_geography.elevation_range: 950–1,200 ft. The Raccoon River valley near Jefferson drops to approximately 960 ft; the upland surface averages 1,100-1,200 ft. Relief along the river reaches 150-200 feet.
physical_geography.dominant_terrain: Gently rolling glacial till plain dissected by the Raccoon River and its tributaries. The terrain is slightly more rolling than the counties to the north, with better natural drainage on the slopes and poorly drained flats between the drainage divides. The Raccoon River valley is broad and well-wooded, providing a landscape contrast to the open prairie uplands.

soil_types.description: The standard Des Moines Lobe catena on the uplands. Clarion on the knolls, Nicollet on the side slopes, Webster in the depressions.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Highly productive once drained. Greene County's slightly better natural drainage than Calhoun or Sac counties meant lower tile costs and earlier settlement.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Greene County, Iowa. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1929; revised 2003.

soil_types.description: Deep silt loam formed in thick loess on the uplands. Well-drained and highly productive.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Excellent corn and soybean soil. The Galva series required less drainage investment than the Webster soils, making it preferred cropland.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Greene County, Iowa (1929; rev. 2003).

major_waterways.name: North Raccoon River
major_waterways.role: Primary drainage, mill power, settlement corridor, timber source
major_waterways.description: The North Raccoon River crosses Greene County from northwest to southeast, joining the Middle Raccoon River near the county's southeastern corner. The river valley is broad and well-wooded — the primary timber source in a county that was otherwise open prairie. Jefferson was established on Hardin Creek near its junction with the Raccoon.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The Raccoon River at Jefferson was the county's lifeline — mill power, timber for building, and the only place in a prairie county where a family could find a tree big enough to shade the house.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Tallgrass prairie with gallery forest along the Raccoon River
vegetation_eras.description: Tallgrass prairie covered the uplands. The Raccoon River valley supported dense gallery forest — cottonwood, bur oak, elm, walnut, and hickory — that was the only significant timber in the county. Prairie fires, some likely set by the Ioway people, maintained the open grassland. The contrast between the treeless prairie upland and the wooded river valley was dramatic.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Prairie broken for cultivation; river valley timber harvested for building
vegetation_eras.description: Settlers claimed quarter-sections on the upland prairie but depended on the river valley for timber, fuel, and shelter during the first years. Prairie breaking proceeded from the 1850s through the 1870s. By 1880, most of the upland was under cultivation. Farmstead windbreaks — Osage orange hedgerows, cottonwood and box elder groves — were planted to break the wind on the treeless uplands.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: July in Greene County was two landscapes in one — the flat corn prairie on the uplands, stretching unbroken to the horizon under a sky so big it dwarfed everything beneath it, and the Raccoon River valley below, wooded and cool, where the sound of the water and the shade of the cottonwoods offered relief from a heat that built day after day until the thunderstorms broke it. The corn on the uplands was head-high by the Fourth of July, and the county fair at Jefferson drew families from every township — livestock judging, horse racing, and the political speeches that were the entertainment of a pre-radio age. The smell was corn pollen, cut hay, and horse — everything in the county ran on animal power, and the horse was the engine that made it work.
sensory_snapshots.source: Schwieder, Iowa: The Middle Land. Federal Writers' Project, Iowa.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: October in Greene County was the sound of corn hitting the bang board — ears stripped by hand from the dried stalks and thrown against the far side of the wagon, a steady percussion that carried across the flat fields from dawn to dark. The sky was pale blue and enormous, and the dust from the dry corn leaves hung in the air. Jefferson's courthouse square was busy on Saturdays — farmers in town for supplies, the implement dealers displaying next year's equipment, and the cream trucks loading at the cooperative. The Raccoon River valley was in full color — the cottonwoods gold, the oaks bronze, the undergrowth a tangle of red sumac and yellow willow. The farm depression was biting — corn prices had collapsed in 1920, and the land that had been mortgaged at wartime values was underwater. But the corn still had to be picked, and the rhythm of October did not change because the price was wrong.
sensory_snapshots.source: Schwieder, Iowa: The Middle Land. Ross, Iowa Agriculture.

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: January in Greene County was white, flat, and cold — the snow covering the stubble fields, the wind driving it into drifts that blocked the gravel roads, and the temperature dropping to fifteen below on clear nights. Jefferson's Main Street was quiet — the movie theater and the coffee shop were the social centers, and the implement dealers used the winter months to repair equipment and promote spring sales. The farms ran on a reduced schedule — livestock feeding, milking where dairy cattle were kept, and the maintenance work that filled the short days. The corn cribs were full from a harvest that had finally, after the Depression and the war, returned a profit, and the hybrid corn that Iowa State had developed was pushing yields toward 80 bushels per acre — twice what the open-pollinated varieties had managed. The farmer's boy was deciding whether to come back to the farm or take the GI Bill to Iowa State or Drake.
sensory_snapshots.source: Ross, Iowa Agriculture. Schwieder, Iowa: The Middle Land.

landscape_changes.change: Prairie settlement and county seat establishment
landscape_changes.description: Greene County was settled in the 1850s-1870s by a mix of Yankee migrants from the eastern states, German immigrants, and Scandinavians. Jefferson was established as the county seat in 1856, centered on a courthouse square that became the commercial and civic heart of the county. The prairie was broken with heavy plows and ox teams, and within a generation the tallgrass prairie was gone, replaced by corn, oats, and hay.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: An ancestor who arrived in Greene County in the 1850s-1870s was among the first generation to break this specific prairie — a transformative act that converted a landscape of grass into a landscape of grain within a single lifetime.

landscape_changes.change: Corn Belt maturation, Depression, and mechanization
landscape_changes.description: Greene County's agricultural economy matured in the early twentieth century — the corn-hog cycle became the dominant economic rhythm, with corn prices and hog prices driving prosperity or hardship. The farm depression of the 1920s hit hard, and the county experienced significant foreclosures. Recovery came slowly, accelerated by wartime demand. Postwar mechanization — tractors, mechanical corn pickers, hybrid corn — consolidated farms and reduced the rural population. The small crossroads communities declined as the automobile extended the trip to Jefferson.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: The twentieth-century story of Greene County is the story of the Corn Belt itself — a transition from small, diversified, labor-intensive farms to larger, mechanized, specialized operations that produced more corn with fewer people.
landscape_changes.source: Schwieder, Iowa: The Middle Land. Ross, Iowa Agriculture.

narrative_hooks: Greene County was the Corn Belt in its purest form — flat black prairie soil, corn in every direction, and Jefferson's courthouse square at the center of a county that lived and died by the corn price and the hog price.

The Raccoon River valley at Jefferson was the county's secret — a wooded valley in a prairie county where {ancestor_name} could find timber, shade, and a landscape that looked nothing like the flat fields above.

When {ancestor_name} arrived in Greene County in {year}, the prairie was still within reach — the oldest neighbors remembered the grass that stood taller than a man on horseback, and the soil that {ancestor_name} plowed had never been turned before.

October in Greene County was the sound of corn hitting the bang board — {ancestor_name} stripped ears from the stalks by hand, threw them against the wagon side, and measured the year's income in bushels per acre.

The GI Bill gave {ancestor_name}'s son a choice that no previous generation had — stay on the farm or go to college, and the decision reshaped not just a family but a county, because the sons who left took their labor with them.
source_references: Prior, Jean C. Landforms of Iowa. Iowa City: University of Iowa Press, 1991.
Schwieder, Dorothy. Iowa: The Middle Land. Ames: Iowa State University Press, 1996.
Ross, Earle D. Iowa Agriculture: An Historical Survey. Iowa City: State Historical Society of Iowa, 1951.
USDA Soil Survey of Greene County, Iowa. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1929; revised 2003.
Federal Writers' Project. Iowa: A Guide to the Hawkeye State. New York: Viking Press, 1938.
